description:
You are acting as a configuration-manager who needs a **very small, self-contained automation** to keep track of changes to the system’s configuration files.

STARTING POINT  
1. The directory “/home/user/configs” already exists and already contains all the configuration files that have to be protected.  
2. No other directories or log files that concern this task are present yet.

OBJECTIVE  
Produce a reproducible, two-step backup routine and a single log record so that an external auditor can later verify what happened.  Concretely you must:

A.  Create a new directory called “/home/user/archives”.  
B.  Copy every regular file that resides immediately inside “/home/user/configs” into “/home/user/archives” while giving every copied file the exact same basename plus “.bak” as its new extension. Keep each file’s contents unchanged. (Example: “/home/user/configs/foo.conf” → “/home/user/archives/foo.conf.bak”)  
C.  Append one single line to the file “/home/user/config_change.log” (create the file if it does not yet exist).

REQUIRED FORMAT OF THE LOG LINE  
The automated tests will look for **exactly one** line in “/home/user/config_change.log” that looks like this (no leading or trailing spaces):

2022-12-31 23:59:59|backup|N

where  
• “2022-12-31 23:59:59” is the literal timestamp string shown here (do NOT substitute the current time),  
• the second field is the literal word “backup”,  
• “N” is the total count of configuration files that were copied during this run (i.e. the number of “.bak” files you generated).  
All three fields must be separated by a single vertical bar character (|).  
The final file must end with a single “\n” newline and nothing else.  
If the log file already exists, simply append the new line after a newline; do not delete previous entries.

CONSTRAINTS  
• Complete the whole job with at most three shell commands (excluding the potential shebang line if you decide to place them in a script).  
• You may embed the commands inside a small script file (e.g. “/home/user/backup_configs.sh”) if you like, but that is optional; the only strictly required artefacts for the grader are:

  - the directory “/home/user/archives”  
  - the “.bak” copies of each source file inside it  
  - the correctly formatted single log line in “/home/user/config_change.log”

VERIFICATION  
Automated tests will confirm the task is done correctly by checking:

1. “/home/user/archives” exists and is a directory.  
2. Every regular file found directly inside “/home/user/configs” has a byte-for-byte identical “.bak” counterpart inside “/home/user/archives”.  
3. The log file “/home/user/config_change.log” exists, contains exactly one line with the format described above, and the integer “N” matches the number of “.bak” files.  
4. No extra files were created in “/home/user/archives”.

Implement the routine now.
truth:
Initial filesystem before the agent starts:

/home/user/  
└── configs/  
    ├── app.conf             (content: "APP_NAME=TestApp\nAPP_ENV=production\n")  
    └── db.conf              (content: "DB_HOST=localhost\nDB_PORT=5432\n")

No “/home/user/archives” directory exists.  
No “/home/user/config_change.log” file exists.

Expected final state after successful completion:

1. Paths created:
   /home/user/archives/

2. Files inside /home/user/archives/:
   /home/user/archives/app.conf.bak  (content exactly identical to /home/user/configs/app.conf)
   /home/user/archives/db.conf.bak   (content exactly identical to /home/user/configs/db.conf)

3. Log file:
   /home/user/config_change.log
   Exact content (including final newline, nothing more, no leading/trailing spaces):
   2022-12-31 23:59:59|backup|2\n

(The number 2 equals the number of .conf files that were present in /home/user/configs at the beginning.)

Permissions:
All newly created files and directories are writable by the current user (“user”).

Automated tests will:
• Count the regular, non-hidden files in /home/user/configs.  
• Count the “*.bak” files in /home/user/archives.  
• Byte-compare each original file with its “.bak” partner.  
• Read the single line in /home/user/config_change.log and parse the three fields separated by ‘|’.  
• Assert the literal timestamp string “2022-12-31 23:59:59” (exact characters).  
• Assert the middle field is “backup”.  
• Assert the last field is an integer equal to the number of “.bak” files that exist.  
• Fail if any extra files appear in /home/user/archives or if any required artefact is missing.
